title: Unit 6: Queue
description: Learn about First-In-First-Out (FIFO) structures, including circular arrays and deques.

topics.title: 6.1 Linear Queue
topics.description: A basic FIFO queue implemented using an array, where insertion occurs at the rear and deletion occurs at the front.

complexity.time: O(1) enqueue, O(n) dequeue (due to element shifting) or O(1) with pointer shift

ispo.desc: Accepts integer value to enqueue.
Declares internal array storage, front/rear indexes, and queue capacity.
Declares variable to hold dequeued item value.
Verifies boundary limits and appends item at rear pointer index, shifting it right.
Verifies empty state and increments front pointer to shift active index.
Prints queue full error message.
Prints queue empty error message.
Returns dequeued value.

quiz.q: What is the main limitation of a simple Linear Queue implemented with shifting front pointers?
o: Array indices before 'front' are wasted and cannot be reused, causing false queue-full states

topics.title: 6.2 Stack using Queues
topics.description: Implement a LIFO stack using two FIFO queues. One queue is used to buffer elements and maintain LIFO push/pop order.

complexity.time: O(n) push, O(1) pop (or vice versa)

topics.cheatSheet:
Queue<Integer> q1 = new LinkedList<>(), q2 = new LinkedList<>();
// Push: q2.add(x); while(!q1.isEmpty()) q2.add(q1.poll()); Swap q1 & q2.

ispo.desc: Accepts element x to push onto the stack.
Creates q1 and q2 queue objects to hold element configurations.
Creates temporary queue variable temp used to execute reference swaps.
Queues new item in q2, flushes q1's contents behind it to reverse FIFO ordering, and swaps queue references.
Polls and returns front of q1 (which is the top of the stack).

quiz.q: What is the time complexity of the pop operation in a StackUsingQueues implementation with a costly push?

topics.title: 6.3 Queue using Stacks
topics.description: Implement a FIFO queue using two LIFO stacks. One stack buffers inputs, while the second yields outputs in FIFO order upon pops.
topics.cheatSheet:
Stack<Integer> s1 = new Stack<>(), s2 = new Stack<>();
// Dequeue: if(s2.isEmpty()) while(!s1.isEmpty()) s2.push(s1.pop()); return s2.pop();

ispo.desc: Accepts element x to enqueue.
Allocates inputStack and outputStack variables in memory.
Pops all elements from inputStack and pushes them to outputStack to invert the LIFO ordering if outputStack is empty.
Pops and returns top element from outputStack.

quiz.q: Why is the dequeue operation considered O(1) amortized despite having a while loop?
o: Each element is moved between stacks at most once during its lifetime, yielding a constant average cost per operation
Loops in Java run in constant time

topics.title: 6.4 Circular Queue (Array-based)
topics.description: A queue implementation using a fixed-size array where the last position connects back to the first to avoid wasted space.
topics.cheatSheet:
Enqueue: rear = (rear + 1) % size; arr[rear] = val;
Dequeue: val = arr[front]; front = (front + 1) % size;

ispo.desc: Takes integer value to add to circular queue.
Allocates queueArray and front/rear index indicators.
Allocates size of queueArray in memory.
Allocates temporary variable value to store data being removed.
Moves rear pointer circularly using modulo and assigns value.
Checks boundaries, reads front value, and increments front pointer circularly.
Returns true to signify successful enqueue.
Returns the dequeued value.

quiz.q: What operator makes index increments circular in an array queue?

topics.title: 6.5 Deque (Doubly Ended Queue)
topics.description: A double-ended queue supporting element insertion and deletion at both the front and rear endpoints.

ispo.desc: Allocates an ArrayDeque object in memory.
Stores removed values in frontVal and rearVal.
Performs operations adding elements to the front and back of the deque.
Pulls elements off the front and back of the deque.
Prints current state of Deque.
Prints popped values.

quiz.q: Which data structures can be represented or fully implemented by a Deque?
